Grounds (claim / SPOV): STAAR trait structure (Org & Dev 0-3, Conv 0-2); dual-rater scoring, 10-point maximum

Grounds (claim / SPOV): ACT Writing 40-minute prompt (three perspectives); 4 analytic domains (Ideas and Analysis, Development and Support, Organization, Language Use and Conventions), each scored 1-6 by two raters, reported 2-12

Grounds (claim / SPOV): SBAC G11 full-write rubric (Org/Purpose 1-4, Evidence/Elaboration 1-4, Conventions 0-2); argument and explanatory modes (no narrative G11)

Primary Source: Massachusetts Department of Elementary and Secondary Education, MCAS Grade 10 Test Design
Grounds (claim / SPOV): MCAS G10 text-based essay (8-point rubric)

Primary Source: Florida Department of Education, B.E.S.T. Writing Argumentation Rubric
Grounds (claim / SPOV): FL B.E.S.T. G7-10 argumentation rubric; gating rule (no citation caps Development at 2/4)

Primary Source: Judith Hochman & Natalie Wexler, The Writing Revolution (TWR)
Grounds (claim / SPOV): Sentence-level moves (because/but/so, appositives, subordinate clauses); explicit teaching of syntactic choices; kernel-expansion pedagogy
Reachable via: *The Writing Revolution* (Hochman & Wexler, 2017); thewritingrevolution.org; Natalie Wexler on X (@natwexler) and *The Knowledge Gap*.

Primary Source: Gerald Graff & Cathy Birkenstein, They Say / I Say (TSIS)
Grounds (claim / SPOV): Meta-commentary templates ("They say X, I say Y"); framing, conceding, responding moves; counterargument moves
Reachable via: *They Say / I Say* (Graff & Birkenstein, 5th ed.); wwnorton.com.

Primary Source: Debra Myhill
Grounds (claim / SPOV): Grammar-as-choice paradigm (stylistic manipulation of sentence structure); Exeter research on contextualized grammar instruction
Reachable via: *Essential Primary Grammar* (Myhill); Exeter grammar-as-choice research; Google Scholar: Debra Myhill.

Primary Source: Jeff Anderson
Grounds (claim / SPOV): Mentor-text analysis (noticing author's choices); sentence imitation and manipulation; "What do you notice?" inquiry approach
Reachable via: *Mechanically Inclined*, *Everyday Editing* (Anderson); Jeff Anderson on X (@writeguyjeff).

Primary Source: Siegfried Engelmann, Theory of Instruction (Direct Instruction)
Grounds (claim / SPOV): Discrimination-before-production pedagogy; minimal-pair contrasts; explicit sequence design. **(Grade-C / design bet, unvalidated for writing)** The discrimination-before-production principle is grounded in Engelmann's work on concept acquisition, but its application to essay-level compositional moves in this course is a design bet, not validated by direct empirical evidence in writing pedagogy.
Reachable via: *Theory of Instruction* (Engelmann & Carnine); National Institute for Direct Instruction (nifdi.org).

Primary Source: Barak Rosenshine, Principles of Instruction
Grounds (claim / SPOV): Check for understanding (CFU) after each small step; guided practice before independent practice; systematic review

Primary Source: Paul Kirschner, Carl Hendrick & Jim Heal, How Learning Happens
Grounds (claim / SPOV): Cognitive load theory (novices need worked examples, not open-ended discovery); split-attention effect; expertise reversal effect (d=-0.43)
Reachable via: *How Learning Happens* (Kirschner, Hendrick & Heal, 2020); Kirschner on X (@P_A_Kirschner).

Primary Source: Steve Graham & Karen Harris, Self-Regulated Strategy Development (SRSD)
Grounds (claim / SPOV): SRSD lesson shell (Develop Background Knowledge, Discuss, Model, Memorize, Support, Independent Practice); mnemonic strategy instruction; explicit teaching of planning and revision strategies
Reachable via: srsdonline.org; Graham & Perin, "Writing Next" (2007); *Powerful Writing Strategies for All Students* (Harris, Graham, Mason & Friedlander, 2008); Google Scholar: Steve Graham (100,000+ citations).

Primary Source: Paul Kirschner, Carl Hendrick & Jim Heal, How Learning Happens
Grounds (claim / SPOV): Expertise reversal effect (scaffolding helps novices, d=+0.43; hurts experts, d=-0.43); fading protocol (withdraw worked examples as competence grows)
Reachable via: *How Learning Happens* (Kirschner, Hendrick & Heal, 2020); Kirschner on X (@P_A_Kirschner).

Primary Source: Barry Zimmerman
Grounds (claim / SPOV): Self-regulated learning (SRL) progression (observation, emulation, self-control, self-regulation); metacognitive monitoring; gradual independence
Reachable via: Google Scholar: Barry Zimmerman (60,000+ citations); *Handbook of Self-Regulation of Learning and Performance* (Routledge, 2011).

Primary Source: Dylan Wiliam & John Hattie
Grounds (claim / SPOV): Formative assessment and self-assessment calibration; rubric internalization; feedback loops
Reachable via: *Embedded Formative Assessment* (Wiliam); *Visible Learning* (Hattie, 2009); Wiliam on X (@dylanwiliam).

Primary Source: Steve Graham & Karen Harris, SRSD
Grounds (claim / SPOV): SRSD fading sequence (Develop → Discuss → Model → Memorize → Support → Independent); gradual release of responsibility; transfer tasks
Reachable via: srsdonline.org; Graham & Perin, "Writing Next" (2007); *Powerful Writing Strategies for All Students* (Harris, Graham, Mason & Friedlander, 2008); Google Scholar: Steve Graham (100,000+ citations).

Primary Source: David Yeager
Grounds (claim / SPOV): Adolescent motivation and growth mindset; self-transcendent purpose; mastery vs. performance goals; developmental context for G9-12 learners

Primary Source: Pietro Boscolo & Suzanne Hidi
Grounds (claim / SPOV): Writing motivation and interest; task value and self-efficacy; engagement in composition tasks
Reachable via: Hidi & Boscolo, "Motivation and writing" (in *Handbook of Writing Research*); Boscolo & Hidi, *Writing and Motivation* (2007).

Primary Source: George Hillocks, Research on Written Composition
Grounds (claim / SPOV): Grammar-in-isolation has near-zero effect on writing quality (d=-0.29 in 1986 meta-analysis); contextualized grammar instruction within real composition is effective

Primary Source: Debra Myhill
Grounds (claim / SPOV): Grammar-as-choice paradigm (stylistic manipulation, not error-correction drills); Exeter research showing gains when grammar is taught as rhetorical resource in composition
Reachable via: *Essential Primary Grammar* (Myhill); Exeter grammar-as-choice research; Google Scholar: Debra Myhill.

Primary Source: Common Core State Standards (CCSS), Language Standards L.9-10.1-2 and L.11-12.1-2

Primary Source: Texas TEKS, Conventions Standards
Grounds (claim / SPOV): TEKS conventions scope (punctuation, capitalization, spelling, grammar) for English I/II; state-specific progression

Primary Source: Deborah McCutchen, "A capacity theory of writing: Working memory in composition"
Grounds (claim / SPOV): Writing capacity theory (transcription, text generation, executive processes compete for limited working memory); novices struggle with simultaneous demands; supports chunking and fading
Reachable via: McCutchen, "A capacity theory of writing: Working memory in composition" (*Educational Psychology Review*, 1996); Google Scholar: Deborah McCutchen.

Primary Source: John Hayes & Linda Flower
Grounds (claim / SPOV): Cognitive process model of writing (planning, translating, reviewing); recursive processes; expert-novice differences (experts plan more, revise more globally)
Reachable via: Hayes & Flower, "A Cognitive Process Theory of Writing" (1981); Hayes, "A new framework for understanding cognition and affect in writing" (1996).

Primary Source: Grant Wiggins & Jay McTighe, Understanding by Design (UbD)
Grounds (claim / SPOV): Backward design (start with end-of-course assessment, design transfer tasks first, then build prerequisite skills); transfer as the goal
